questions.text: In Step 1 of the ER-to-Relational mapping, what is created for each regular (strong) entity type E?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A foreign key in a related relation

choices.label: B
choices.text: A relation R that includes all simple attributes of E

choices.label: C
choices.text: A new relation for each attribute of E

choices.label: D
choices.text: A surrogate key for the entity

questions.explanation: According to Step 1, for each regular entity type, a relation R is created containing all simple attributes of that entity.

questions.text: If a regular entity type has a composite key, how is the primary key of the resulting relation formed in Step 1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A single new attribute is created to replace it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Only the first simple attribute of the composite key is used

choices.label: C
choices.text: The set of simple attributes that form the composite key together form the primary key

choices.label: D
choices.text: Composite keys are not allowed in relational mapping

questions.explanation: Step 1 specifies that if the chosen key is composite, the set of simple attributes forming it will together form the primary key of the relation.

questions.text: Which of the following is true regarding the mapping of weak entity types (Step 2)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They do not require their own relation

choices.label: B
choices.text: The relation includes the primary key of the owner entity as a foreign key

choices.label: C
choices.text: The primary key is the same as the owner entity's primary key

choices.label: D
choices.text: Weak entities only contain their partial keys

questions.explanation: Step 2 states that for a weak entity, the relation must include the primary key of the owner entity type as a foreign key.

questions.text: In the mapping of the weak entity type **DEPENDENT**, what is the primary key of the resulting relation?
questions.explanation: The primary key is the combination of the owner's primary key (ESSN) and the weak entity's partial key (DEPENDENT_NAME).

questions.text: In Step 3, when mapping a binary 1:1 relationship type using the Foreign Key approach, which relation is it better to choose to include the foreign key?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Either relation, it makes no difference

choices.label: D
choices.text: The relation with the most attributes

questions.explanation: Step 3 suggests it is better to choose an entity type with total participation to serve in the role of the relation containing the foreign key.

questions.text: According to the example in Step 3, why is the **DEPARTMENT** relation chosen to include the foreign key for the **MANAGES** relationship?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because every employee manages a department

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because its participation in the relationship is total

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because it has fewer attributes than EMPLOYEE

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because it is a strong entity

questions.explanation: The text states DEPARTMENT is chosen because its participation in the MANAGES relationship type is total.

questions.text: When mapping a binary 1:N relationship (Step 4), where should the foreign key be placed?

choices.label: B
choices.text: In the relation representing the N-side

questions.explanation: Step 4 specifies including the primary key of the 1-side relation as a foreign key in the N-side relation (S).

questions.text: In the example of the **WORKS_FOR** relationship, which attribute is added to the **EMPLOYEE** relation?
questions.explanation: For WORKS_FOR (a 1:N relationship), the primary key DNUMBER of DEPARTMENT is included as foreign key DNO in the EMPLOYEE relation.

questions.text: How are simple attributes of a 1:N relationship type handled in Step 4?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are included as attributes of the relation on the 1-side

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are included as attributes of the relation on the N-side

choices.label: D
choices.text: A new relation is created for them

questions.explanation: Step 4 states to include any simple attributes of the 1:N relation type as attributes of S (the N-side relation).

questions.text: Which step requires the creation of a new relation S to represent the relationship itself?
questions.explanation: Step 5 for M:N relationships requires creating a new relation S to represent the relationship.

questions.text: In the mapping of the **WORKS_ON** M:N relationship, what is the primary key of the resulting relation?
questions.explanation: The primary key of the WORKS_ON relation is the combination of the foreign keys referencing the participating entities (ESSN and PNO).

questions.text: What is the result of mapping a multivalued attribute A according to Step 6?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A new column is added to the original relation

choices.label: B
choices.text: A new relation R is created including A and the primary key of the parent entity

choices.label: C
choices.text: The attribute is ignored in the relational model

choices.label: D
choices.text: A comma-separated list is stored in a single field

questions.explanation: Step 6 states to create a new relation R that includes the attribute A plus the primary key of the parent entity as a foreign key.

questions.text: In mapping the **LOCATIONS** multivalued attribute of **DEPARTMENT**, what is the primary key of the **DEPT_LOCATIONS** relation?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A combination of DNAME and DLOCATION

questions.explanation: The primary key of the relation created for a multivalued attribute is the combination of the attribute itself and the foreign key referencing the parent entity.

questions.text: For an n-ary relationship type where n > 2 (Step 7), how is the primary key of the new relation S determined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the primary key of the first participating entity

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a combination of all primary keys of the participating entity types

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a new surrogate key

choices.label: D
choices.text: It depends on the participation constraints

questions.explanation: Step 7 states that the primary key of the relation representing an n-ary relationship is usually the combination of the foreign keys of the participating entity types.

questions.text: In the mapping of the ternary relationship **SUPPLY**, which attributes form the primary key?
questions.explanation: According to the Step 7 example, the primary key of the relation SUPPLY is the combination of the three foreign keys {SNAME, PARTNO, PROJNAME}.

questions.text: Based on Table 7.1, what does a 'Multivalued attribute' in the ER model correspond to in the Relational Model?
questions.explanation: Table 7.1 lists 'Relation and foreign key' as the relational correspondence for a Multivalued attribute.

questions.text: Based on Table 7.1, what is the relational equivalent of an 'Entity type'?
questions.explanation: Table 7.1 defines the correspondence of an Entity type as an “Entity” relation.

questions.text: How is a composite attribute handled during ER-to-Relational mapping according to Table 7.1?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is mapped as a single string attribute

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is mapped as a set of simple component attributes

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is mapped to a separate relation

questions.explanation: Table 7.1 states that a Composite attribute corresponds to a 'Set of simple component attributes'.

questions.text: Which option for mapping specialization/generalization involves creating a relation for the superclass and separate relations for each subclass?
questions.explanation: Option 8A is described as 'Multiple relations—Superclass and subclasses'.

questions.text: In Option 8A, what is the primary key of the subclass relations?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A new unique ID for each subclass

choices.label: B
choices.text: The same primary key as the superclass relation

choices.label: D
choices.text: A combination of the subclass name and the superclass key

questions.explanation: In Option 8A, PK(Li) = k, where k is the primary key of the superclass relation L.

questions.text: For which type of specialization is Option 8B (Subclass relations only) strictly appropriate?
questions.explanation: Option 8B description states: 'This option only works for a specialization whose subclasses are total'.

questions.text: In Option 8C, what is the purpose of the attribute 't'?

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a boolean flag for total participation

questions.explanation: Option 8C uses attribute 't' as a type or discriminating attribute to indicate which subclass each tuple belongs to.

questions.text: Which mapping option is most suitable for overlapping subclasses in a single relation?
questions.explanation: Option 8D (Single relation with multiple type attributes) uses boolean attributes \( t_i \) to indicate membership in potentially multiple (overlapping) subclasses.

questions.text: What is a 'surrogate key' in the context of mapping Union Types (Categories)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A key inherited from the first superclass

choices.label: B
choices.text: A new key attribute specified for the category relation

choices.label: C
choices.text: A foreign key pointing to all superclasses

choices.label: D
choices.text: A key that must be a composite of all superclass keys

questions.explanation: Step 9 explains that for union types with different keys, it is customary to specify a new key attribute called a surrogate key.

questions.text: In the example of the **OWNER** category, what is the surrogate key called?
questions.explanation: Step 35 mentions creating a relation OWNER with a surrogate key called OwnerId.

questions.text: What is multiple inheritance in the context of EER mapping?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A subclass belonging to more than one superclass

choices.label: D
choices.text: An entity having multiple primary keys

questions.explanation: A shared subclass (like STUDENT_ASSISTANT) is a subclass of several classes, indicating multiple inheritance.

questions.text: In shared subclasses (multiple inheritance), what is the requirement regarding key attributes of the superclasses?

choices.label: B
choices.text: They must all have the same key attribute

choices.label: C
choices.text: They must all be composite keys

choices.label: D
choices.text: There are no restrictions on keys

questions.explanation: The text states: 'These classes must all have the same key attribute; otherwise, the shared subclass would be modeled as a category'.

questions.text: Which options can be applied to map a shared subclass (multiple inheritance)?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Any of the options discussed in Step 8 (subject to restrictions)

questions.explanation: Step 37 states: 'We can apply any of the options discussed in Step 8 to a shared subclass'.

questions.text: In the relational schema result for the **COMPANY** ER diagram, what relation represents the **LOCATIONS** multivalued attribute of **DEPARTMENT**?
questions.explanation: Example 18 mentions the creation of the relation DEPT_LOCATIONS for this purpose.

questions.text: What constitutes the primary key of a relation S created for a binary M:N relationship R?

choices.label: B
choices.text: The combination of foreign keys referencing the participating entity types

choices.label: C
choices.text: The primary key of the 'Many' side

choices.label: D
choices.text: The simple attributes of the relationship

questions.explanation: Step 5 states: 'Include as foreign key attributes in S the primary keys of the relations that represent the participating entity types; their combination will form the primary key of S'.

questions.text: Under Option 8C, if an entity belongs to subclass \( S_2 \) but not \( S_1 \), what happens to the attributes of \( S_1 \) in that tuple?

choices.label: C
choices.text: The tuple is split into two relations

choices.label: D
choices.text: This situation is not possible in 8C

questions.explanation: While not explicitly stated in the summary text, Option 8C creates a single relation with all subclass attributes; logically, attributes of subclasses to which the entity does not belong would be NULL.

questions.text: Which step in the mapping algorithm handles Union Types (Categories)?
questions.explanation: Step 9 is dedicated to the mapping of Union Types (Categories).

questions.text: In Step 5, what attribute of the **WORKS_ON** relation represents the hours worked?
questions.explanation: Step 16 states: 'Attribute HOURS in WORKS_ON represents the HOURS attribute of the relation type'.

questions.text: Which of the following mapping options for specialization works for ANY specialization (total or partial, disjoint or overlapping)?
questions.explanation: Step 26 states that Option 8A 'works for any specialization (total or partial, disjoint of overlapping)'.

questions.text: What is the primary key of the **EMPLOYEE** relation in the mapping example provided?
questions.explanation: Step 6 mentions that 'SSN, DNUMBER, and PNUMBER are the primary keys for the relations EMPLOYEE, DEPARTMENT, and PROJECT'.

questions.text: How many foreign keys would a relation created for a ternary (3-ary) relationship type typically have?
questions.explanation: According to Step 7 and Table 7.1, an n-ary relationship maps to a relation with n foreign keys.

questions.text: In mapping a 1:1 relationship, if one side has partial participation and the other has total participation, which approach is recommended?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the Foreign Key in the relation with total participation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Place the Foreign Key in the relation with partial participation

questions.explanation: Step 10 states: 'It is better to choose an entity type with total participation in R in the role of S' (the relation containing the foreign key).

questions.text: True or False: In Option 8D, each subclass is represented by a Boolean attribute.
questions.explanation: Step 32 states: 'Each \( t_i \) ... is a Boolean type attribute indicating whether a tuple belongs to the subclass \( S_i \)'.

questions.text: In the relational model correspondence for a key attribute (Table 7.1), what is it mapped to?
questions.explanation: Table 7.1 lists 'Primary (or secondary) key' as the correspondence for a 'Key attribute'.

questions.text: When mapping a Union Type (Category) where superclasses have different keys, what is Step 9's custom recommendation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Use the key of the first superclass only

choices.label: B
choices.text: Create a composite key from all superclasses

choices.label: C
choices.text: Specify a new surrogate key attribute

choices.label: D
choices.text: Convert the category back into a standard entity

questions.explanation: Step 33 states: 'it is customary to specify a new key attribute, called a surrogate key, when creating a relation to correspond to the category'.
